summary: Student data privacy. Requires every NY school district to post a Parents Bill of Rights for Data Privacy, sign Data Protection Agreements with every third-party vendor handling student personally identifiable information, designate a Data Protection Officer, and maintain a public inventory of vendors.
key_provisions: Parents Bill of Rights for Data Privacy must be posted publicly
Every vendor with access to student PII must have a signed Data Protection Agreement (DPA)
Parents have the right to inspect and request deletion of their child's data
Districts must report data breaches to the state and to affected parents
Each district must designate a Data Protection Officer (DPO)
Districts must maintain a public inventory of third-party data-processing vendors

full_citation: 8 NYCRR Part 121 (Strengthening Data Privacy and Security in New York State Educational Agencies)
summary: The implementing regulation for §2-d. Spells out what districts must publish, what every DPA must contain, what training is required for staff, and what the Parents Bill of Rights must include.
key_provisions: Specifies the minimum content of every DPA between a district and a third-party vendor
Requires annual data privacy and security training for staff with access to PII
Specifies the timeline and content of breach notifications
Requires posting a Supplemental Information document for each vendor DPA

full_citation: Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act, 20 U.S.C. § 1232g
summary: Federal law governing access to and disclosure of education records. Parents have the right to inspect their child's records, request corrections, and consent to most disclosures of personally identifiable information.
key_provisions: Right to inspect and review education records
Right to request amendment of records believed to be inaccurate
Generally, written consent required before disclosure of PII from records
Limited exceptions, including for 'school officials with legitimate educational interest'

full_citation: Children's Online Privacy Protection Act, 15 U.S.C. §§ 6501–6506
summary: Federal law restricting the online collection of personal information from children under 13. Schools may consent on behalf of parents only in limited circumstances tied to legitimate educational use.
key_provisions: Verifiable parental consent generally required before collecting PII from children under 13
Schools may provide consent only when use is for an educational purpose authorized by the school
Operators must provide notice and a means for parents to review and delete data

full_citation: Chapter 56 of the Laws of 2025, Education Law § 814
summary: Requires NY school districts to adopt policies restricting student use of personal smartphones and personal internet-enabled devices during the school day. The statute applies to personal devices; school-issued devices used for instruction are excluded from its restrictions. The law is not a 1:1 device mandate.
key_provisions: Districts must adopt a written policy restricting personal smartphones and similar devices during instructional time
School-issued devices used for instruction are excluded from the personal-device restriction
The law does not require districts to issue 1:1 devices
